title: HVAC Preventive Maintenance Checklists (Homeowner vs Professional)
intended_use: Actionable preventive maintenance guidance for homeowners and HVAC technicians. Clear safety boundaries: homeowner-safe tasks vs professional-only work.

always_emphasize: If you smell gas, see smoke, hear arcing/sparking, or suspect carbon monoxide: stop and seek emergency help immediately.
Homeowners should not open electrical compartments, handle capacitors, or work with refrigerants or gas valves.
Always turn the system OFF at the thermostat before any homeowner cleaning tasks; never touch wiring.
stop_and_call_pro_triggers: CO alarm sounding or flu-like symptoms that improve outdoors
Ice buildup on refrigerant lines that returns after filter/airflow corrections
Gas smell near furnace or outdoor meter

roles.homeowner: Safe DIY tasks only (no panels opened, no wiring touched).
roles.technician: Licensed/qualified HVAC technician performing electrical, refrigerant, combustion, and performance testing.

homeowner_maintenance_safe_diy.overview: Simple routine checks reduce breakdowns and help catch problems early. If anything looks unsafe or abnormal, stop and call a professional.

steps: Turn system OFF at thermostat.
Locate filter (return grille, filter cabinet, or air handler slot).
Check for visible dirt buildup - if you cannot see light through it, replace it.
Replace with the same size; align airflow arrow toward equipment.
Turn system back ON and confirm normal airflow.
notes: Standard 1-inch filters typically need replacement every 30-90 days.
If you have pets, construction dust, or allergies, expect more frequent replacement.
If replacing the filter causes airflow to drop noticeably, the filter may be too restrictive.

steps: Confirm correct mode (COOL/HEAT/AUTO) and setpoint.
Confirm schedule/hold settings match your routine.
Check that the display is responsive.
Look for low battery icon - replace batteries if needed.
Walk around indoor and outdoor units without opening panels.
Look for water around indoor equipment or rust staining.
Look for ice on the refrigerant line or outdoor unit.
Check that outdoor unit has 2-foot clearance and isn't buried in debris.
what_to_watch_for: Water pooling, rust streaks, moldy odors
Oil stains at refrigerant connections (possible leak)

what_sounds_mean.action: Turn system off and call a pro immediately (electrical hazard).
Check for loose panels/nearby debris; call a pro if persistent.

steps: Turn system OFF at thermostat (and allow fan to stop).
Remove leaves/grass around the unit; maintain 2-foot clearance.
Gently rinse exterior fins with a garden hose from the outside (no pressure washer).
Do not spray into electrical compartments or disturb wiring.
spring_cooling_prep.notes: If coils are impacted with heavy grease/dirt, schedule professional coil cleaning.

steps: Look for water around the indoor unit and at the drain termination.
Pour 1 cup of distilled vinegar or warm water into the drain line access port to verify flow.
If safe and accessible, use a wet/dry vacuum at the termination (power OFF).
spring_cooling_prep.notes: If you see repeated overflow, stop and schedule service.

steps: Run AC for 15 minutes on a warm day before the first heat wave.
Confirm steady airflow and expected temperature change.
Note any odors, noises, or short cycling.
when_to_call_pro: Repeated on/off cycling within a few minutes

fall_heating_prep.action: Ensure all supply and return vents are open and not blocked by furniture or rugs.
Replace the water panel (evaporator pad) and turn the humidistat ON.

steps: Run heat for 10-15 minutes before sustained cold arrives.
Note any burning smell (dust burning off is normal for first run).
Confirm warm air at registers.

annual_tasks.frequency: Annually (or twice/year for heat + cool climates)
annual_tasks.action: Book spring AC check and fall furnace check.
annual_tasks.notes: Best practice is spring (cooling) and fall (heating) to catch issues before peak demand.

annual_tasks.action: Replace batteries in thermostats, Carbon Monoxide detectors, and Smoke detectors.
annual_tasks.notes: If devices are hardwired with battery backup, still replace backup batteries.

steps: Vacuum visible dust from grilles.
Ensure furniture/rugs aren't blocking returns or supplies.
Confirm each room has adequate return pathway (doors not tightly sealed without transfer).

professional_maintenance_cooling.overview: Cooling tune-ups focus on coil condition, electrical reliability, airflow, condensate safety, and confirming performance.

outdoor_unit_condenser.purpose: Restore heat rejection and reduce compressor stress.
outdoor_unit_condenser.action: Remove dirt/pollen/cottonwood buildup with appropriate cleaner.

outdoor_unit_condenser.purpose: Compare to nameplate ratings to detect motor stress.

notes: Only hook up gauges if necessary.
If low charge is found, leak detection/repair is the priority - not just adding refrigerant.

checks: Recommend appropriate MERV based on system static pressure

system_performance_checks.notes: Interpretation depends on indoor humidity, airflow, and outdoor conditions. Not a standalone diagnostic.

system_performance_checks.purpose: Detect airflow restriction, undersized ducts, and filter issues.
system_performance_checks.notes: High static pressure causes noise, poor comfort, and premature motor failures.

professional_maintenance_heating.overview: Heating tune-ups prioritize safety (combustion/CO), venting, ignition reliability, and heat pump defrost functionality.

safety_critical.purpose: Detect failure risk and CO hazards.
safety_critical.action: Check for cracks, holes, or rust.
safety_critical.notes: Inspection requires training and appropriate tools; do not rely on visual-only shortcuts.

safety_critical.action: Soap test gas piping and valve connections.

heat_pump_heating_mode.purpose: Confirm outdoor coil defrost operates and clears ice reliably.
heat_pump_heating_mode.action: Force defrost to ensure sensors/board work.

heat_pump_heating_mode.purpose: Ensure backup heat functions and staging is correct.

types_and_merv.use: Basic equipment protection only. Catches lint/large dust.
Good balance. Catches pollen, mites, mold spores. Standard for most homes.

cons: Can restrict airflow if system/ducts are not designed for it
types_and_merv.warning: Can raise static pressure and harm comfort/equipment if not compatible with system.

pros: Excellent filtration for smoke, virus carriers
types_and_merv.warning: CRITICAL: Do not use 1-inch MERV 13+ filters in standard systems. High restriction can freeze coils and kill compressors. Requires deep pleat (4-5 inch) media cabinet or dedicated filtration module.

how_to_choose: Start with the equipment and duct system's airflow tolerance (static pressure).
Use a filter that matches the rack size exactly; avoid gaps/bypass.
The 'best' filter is the one that keeps the air clean without choking the equipment.
MERV 8 is the safe standard for 1-inch slots.
For allergies/smoke concerns, consider a thicker media filter cabinet rather than a restrictive 1-inch high-MERV.
If airflow is already weak, do not jump to high MERV without technician verification.

replacement_frequency.notes: Frequency varies by home conditions: pets, smoking, construction dust, occupancy, and runtime.

symptoms.note: System running longer to maintain temperature.

symptoms.sign: Short cycling (unit turns on/off every 5 mins)
Ice visible on copper refrigerant lines
symptoms.note: If it returns after filter/airflow corrections, call a pro.

types: Burning smell = dust on heat strips (normal first run) or electrical issue
Rotten egg smell = gas leak (evacuate immediately)

symptoms.note: Standing water near electrical components is a safety hazard.

symptoms.action: If burning smell or electrical buzzing: stop and call a pro immediately.

whats_typically_included: Two visits per year (Spring AC, Fall Heat)
Priority scheduling (jump the line during heat waves/cold snaps)
Basic cleaning, safety checks, and performance verification
benefits: Prevents 90% of breakdowns by catching issues early
Maintains manufacturer warranty (proof of maintenance often required)
what_to_look_for_in_good_agreement: Clear checklist of what the visit includes
Combustion safety testing for furnaces (CO check)
Proof of measured performance provided to you
red_flags_bad_contractors: 'Tune-up' that is only a quick look with no measurements
Techs who find a 'major part' broken every single visit
Hard-sell tactics for unnecessary add-ons without evidence
Pressure to buy a new system when yours is <10 years old and working
Adding refrigerant every year without fixing the leak
No documentation or vague 'everything looks good' reports
No combustion safety testing on gas furnaces
Won't show you what they found or explain the work

why_it_matters: Finds weak capacitors/contactors before they fail on the hottest day
Ensures coils are clean and airflow is correct for peak efficiency
Techs are available before the summer rush
Catching a weak capacitor now prevents failure in July
Combustion safety checks reduce CO risk before closing windows for winter
Confirms ignition reliability and venting integrity
Heat pump defrost and backup heat can be verified before cold snaps

notes: In year-round climates or heat pump-heavy regions, consider two visits per year (spring + fall).
If the system is older or heavily used, more frequent professional checks can prevent costly failures.

what_to_track: Filter change dates and filter type/MERV
why_records_matter: Warranty Validation: Manufacturers may deny parts warranty without proof of maintenance.
Home Sale: Shows buyers the mechanicals were cared for.
Diagnosis: Helps techs see patterns (e.g., 'we just replaced this capacitor 6 months ago').
Identifies repeating issues (recurring drain clogs, capacitor failures).
simple_record_formats: Sticker log near air handler (filter change dates)
Keep service invoices in a folder (physical or digital)
